title: Assert Cheat Sheet
description: Quick reference for using assert statements in Python and JavaScript.

commands.command: assert a == b, 'a and b should be equal'
commands.description: Basic assert statement to check equality
commands.scenario: Basic assert statement to check equality

commands.command: assert len(items) > 0, 'Items list should not be empty'
commands.description: Assert with a condition and custom message
commands.scenario: Assert with a condition and custom message

commands.command: assert isinstance(obj, MyClass), 'Object must be of type MyClass'

commands.description: Using console.assert to check a condition
commands.scenario: Using console.assert to check a condition

commands.command: console.assert(a === b, 'a and b should be strictly equal');

commands.description: Assert with a custom error message
commands.scenario: Assert with a custom error message

commands.command: for item in items: assert item is not None, 'Item should not be None'
commands.description: Assert in a loop to validate each element
commands.scenario: Assert in a loop to validate each element

commands.command: def process(data): assert isinstance(data, dict), 'Data must be a dictionary'
function getNumber() { let num = calculate(); console.assert(typeof num === 'number', 'Return value should be a number'); return num; }
commands.description: Assert in a function to ensure return type
commands.scenario: Assert in a function to ensure return type

commands.description: Assert that a specific block of code raises a specific exception type in Python testing.
Assert equality for floating-point numbers within a specific decimal precision to avoid rounding errors.
Assert that all elements in a collection match the elements of a provided set, regardless of order.
Perform a deep recursive comparison of object properties and values rather than checking reference equality.
Using context managers or assertion wrappers to attach metadata/traceability to failed assertions in complex workflows.
